repo.description: Vim-fork focused on extensibility and usability
repo.name: neovim

body:
Ref: #35558
A prerequisite of #38525

## Problem
Although our LSP module comes with its own logging mechanism, we lack an API for creating logs for other modules.

## Solution
Create `vim.log` based on `vim.lsp.log`, making it easier for other modules/plugins to create logs.

comment_data.body: Is there a (reasonable) use case for changing the format function after creation? Otherwise I wouldn't expose this (for now).

@@ -3759,6 +3759,113 @@ vim.loader.reset({path})                                  *vim.loader.reset()*
       • {path}  (`string?`) path to reset
 
 
+==============================================================================
+Lua module: vim.log                                                  *vim.log*
+
+The |vim.log| module provides file-backed logger instances.
+
+Use `vim.log.new()` to create a logger, it exposes five writer functions:
+`trace()`, `debug()`, `info()`, `warn()`, and `error()`. They are correspond
+to log levels defined by |vim.log.levels|.
+
+Example: >lua
+    local logger = vim.log.new({
+      name = 'my-plugin',
+      filename = vim.fs.joinpath(vim.fn.stdpath('log'), 'my-plugin.log'),
+    })
+
+    logger.error('request failed', 'timeout')
+    -- This will write a line like the following to the log file:
+    --   [ERROR][2024-01-01 12:00:00] source.lua:123    request failed    timeout
+
+    -- Set the log level to `INFO`, otherwise the `info()` call below would be ignored,
+    -- since the default level is `WARN`.
+    vim.log.set_level(logger, vim.log.levels.INFO)
+    logger.info('starting', { buf = vim.api.nvim_get_current_buf() })
+    -- This will write a line like the following to the log file:
+    --   [INFO][2024-01-01 12:01:00] source.lua:124    starting    { buf = 1 }
+<
+
+You can also provide a custom formatter function to customize the log output
+format.
+
+
+*vim.Logger*
+
+    Fields: ~
+      • {trace}            (`fun(...:any): boolean?`) Writes a message at
+                           `vim.log.levels.TRACE`.
+      • {debug}            (`fun(...:any): boolean?`) Writes a message at
+                           `vim.log.levels.DEBUG`.
+      • {info}             (`fun(...:any): boolean?`) Writes a message at
+                           `vim.log.levels.INFO`.
+      • {warn}             (`fun(...:any): boolean?`) Writes a message at
+                           `vim.log.levels.WARN`.
+      • {error}            (`fun(...:any): boolean?`) Writes a message at
+                           `vim.log.levels.ERROR`.
+      • {new}              (`fun(opts: vim.log.new.Opts): vim.Logger`) See
+                           |Logger:new()|.
+      • {set_level}        (`fun(logger: vim.Logger, level: vim.log.levels)`)
+                           See |Logger:set_level()|.
+      • {get_level}        (`fun(logger: vim.Logger): vim.log.levels`) See
+                           |Logger:get_level()|.
+      • {set_format_func}  (`fun(logger: vim.Logger, func: fun(current_level: vim.log.levels, level: vim.log.levels, ...): string?)`)
+                           See |Logger:set_format_func()|.
+
+
+Logger:get_level({logger})                                *Logger:get_level()*
+    Gets the current log level.
+
+    Parameters: ~
+      • {logger}  (`vim.Logger`) See |vim.Logger|.
+
+    Return: ~
+        (`vim.log.levels`)
+
+Logger:new({opts})                                              *Logger:new()*
+    Creates a logger instance.
+
+    The logger writes formatted messages to a file, using a per-instance log
+    level and formatting function.
+
+    Parameters: ~
+      • {opts}  (`table`) A table with the following fields:
+                • {name} (`string`) Display name used in notifications emitted
+                  by the logger.
+                • {filename} (`string`) Path to the log file.
+                • {current_level}? (`vim.log.levels`, default:
+                  `vim.log.levels.WARN`) Minimum level that will be written.
+                • {format_func}?
+                  (`fun(current_level: vim.log.levels, level: vim.log.levels, ...): string?`)
+                  Formatter used for each log entry. Receives the logger's
+                  current level, the message level, and the values passed to
+                  the writer. Return a string to write an entry, or `nil` to
+                  skip it.
+
+    Return: ~
+        (`vim.Logger`) See |vim.Logger|.
+
+Logger:set_format_func({logger}, {func})            *Logger:set_format_func()*

comment_data.body: What I envision is that these exposed functions will serve as the underlying APIs that support other APIs. A plugin using `vim.log` is unlikely to use a function like `set_format_func` directly; instead, it would likely wrap it simply and return a function like `plugin.log.set_format_func`.

comment_data.body:
In this case, I would simply inline it in `vim.lsp.log.should_log` so we don't have to contaminate the module.

(The reasoning for keeping this as clean as possible is that plugins will vendor it for backward compatibility, i.e., do `local log = vim.log or require('myplugin.log')`.)
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -0,0 +1,311 @@
+--- @brief
+---
+--- The |vim.log| module provides file-backed logger instances.
+---
+--- Use `vim.log.new()` to create a logger, it exposes five writer functions:
+--- `trace()`, `debug()`, `info()`, `warn()`, and `error()`.
+--- They are correspond to log levels defined by |vim.log.levels|.
+---
+--- Example:
+---
+--- ```lua
+--- local logger = vim.log.new({
+---   name = 'my-plugin',
+---   filename = vim.fs.joinpath(vim.fn.stdpath('log'), 'my-plugin.log'),
+--- })
+---
+--- logger.error('request failed', 'timeout')
+--- -- This will write a line like the following to the log file:
+--- --   [ERROR][2024-01-01 12:00:00] source.lua:123    request failed    timeout
+---
+--- -- Set the log level to `INFO`, otherwise the `info()` call below would be ignored,
+--- -- since the default level is `WARN`.
+--- vim.log.set_level(logger, vim.log.levels.INFO)
+--- logger.info('starting', { buf = vim.api.nvim_get_current_buf() })
+--- -- This will write a line like the following to the log file:
+--- --   [INFO][2024-01-01 12:01:00] source.lua:124    starting    { buf = 1 }
+--- ```
+---
+--- You can also provide a custom formatter function to customize the log output format.
+
+---@class vim.Logger
+---
+--- Display name used in notifications emitted by the logger.
+---@field private name string
+---
+--- Path of the log file.
+---@field private filename string
+---
+--- Minimum level that will be written.
+---@field private current_level integer
+---
+--- Function used to format a log entry.
+---@field private format_func fun(current_level: vim.log.levels, level:vim.log.levels, ...): string?
+---
+--- Internal state for the log file handle. `nil` until the file is opened.
+---@field private logfile file*?
+---
+--- Internal state for the log file open error.
+---@field private openerr string?
+---
+--- Writes a message at `vim.log.levels.TRACE`.
+---@field trace fun(...:any): boolean?
+---
+--- Writes a message at `vim.log.levels.DEBUG`.
+---@field debug fun(...:any): boolean?
+---
+--- Writes a message at `vim.log.levels.INFO`.
+---@field info fun(...:any): boolean?
+---
+--- Writes a message at `vim.log.levels.WARN`.
+---@field warn fun(...:any): boolean?
+---
+--- Writes a message at `vim.log.levels.ERROR`.
+---@field error fun(...:any): boolean?
+local Logger = {}
+
+---@enum vim.log.levels
+---@nodoc
+Logger.levels = {
+  TRACE = 0,
+  DEBUG = 1,
+  INFO = 2,
+  WARN = 3,
+  ERROR = 4,
+  OFF = 5,
+}
+
+local level_names = {
+  [0] = 'TRACE',
+  [1] = 'DEBUG',
+  [2] = 'INFO',
+  [3] = 'WARN',
+  [4] = 'ERROR',
+  [5] = 'OFF',
+}
+
+local log_date_format = '%F %H:%M:%S'
+
+--- Default formatter used by |vim.log.new()|.
+---
+--- Formats a message as:
+--- `[LEVEL][YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS] source.lua:line<TAB>arg1<TAB>arg2`
+---
+---@param current_level vim.log.levels
+---@param level vim.log.levels
+---@return string?
+local function default_format_func(current_level, level, ...)
+  if level < current_level then
+    return nil
+  end
+
+  local info = debug.getinfo(2, 'Sl')
+  local header = string.format(
+    '[%s][%s] %s:%s',
+    level_names[level],
+    os.date(log_date_format),
+    info.short_src,
+    info.currentline
+  )
+  local parts = { header }
+  local argc = select('#', ...)
+  for i = 1, argc do
+    local arg = select(i, ...)
+    table.insert(parts, arg == nil and 'nil' or vim.inspect(arg, { newline = ' ', indent = '' }))
+  end
+  return table.concat(parts, '\t') .. '\n'
+end
+
+---@class vim.log.new.Opts
+---@inlinedoc
+---
+--- Display name used in notifications emitted by the logger.
+---@field name string
+--- Path to the log file.
+---@field filename string
+--- Minimum level that will be written.
+--- (default: `vim.log.levels.WARN`)
+---@field current_level? vim.log.levels
+--- Formatter used for each log entry.
+--- Receives the logger's current level, the message level, and the values passed to the writer.
+--- Return a string to write an entry, or `nil` to skip it.
+---@field format_func? fun(current_level: vim.log.levels, level: vim.log.levels, ...): string?
+
+--- Creates a logger instance.
+---
+--- The logger writes formatted messages to a file,
+--- using a per-instance log level and formatting function.
+---
+---@param opts vim.log.new.Opts
+---@return vim.Logger
+function Logger.new(opts)
+  vim.validate('opts', opts, 'table')
+  vim.validate('opts.name', opts.name, 'string')
+  vim.validate('opts.filename', opts.filename, 'string')
+  vim.validate('opts.current_level', opts.current_level, 'number', true)
+  vim.validate('opts.format_func', opts.format_func, 'function', true)
+
+  local log_dir = vim.fs.dirname(opts.filename)
+  if log_dir then
+    -- TODO: Ideally the directory should be created in open_file(), right before
+    -- opening the log file, but open() can be called from libuv callbacks,
+    -- where using fn.mkdir() is not allowed.
+    vim.fn.mkdir(log_dir, 'p')
+  end
+
+  local logger = setmetatable({
+    name = opts.name,
+    filename = opts.filename,
+    current_level = opts.current_level or Logger.levels.WARN,
+    format_func = opts.format_func or default_format_func,
+  }, { __index = Logger })
+  logger.trace = logger:create_writer(Logger.levels.TRACE)
+  logger.debug = logger:create_writer(Logger.levels.DEBUG)
+  logger.info = logger:create_writer(Logger.levels.INFO)
+  logger.warn = logger:create_writer(Logger.levels.WARN)
+  logger.error = logger:create_writer(Logger.levels.ERROR)
+
+  return logger
+end
+
+---@param msg string
+---@param level? vim.log.levels
+local function notify(msg, level)
+  if vim.in_fast_event() then
+    vim.schedule(function()
+      vim.notify(msg, level)
+    end)
+  else
+    vim.notify(msg, level)
+  end
+end
+
+--- Opens the log file on first use.
+---
+--- Writes a `[START]` marker when the file is opened successfully.
+---
+---@package
+---@return boolean # `true` if the file is open, `false` on error.
+function Logger:open_file()
+  if self.logfile then
+    return true
+  end
+  if self.openerr then
+    return false
+  end
+
+  self.logfile, self.openerr = io.open(self.filename, 'a+')
+  if not self.logfile then
+    local err_msg = string.format('Failed to open %s log file: %s', self.name, self.openerr)
+    notify(err_msg, Logger.levels.ERROR)
+    return false
+  end
+
+  local log_info = vim.uv.fs_stat(self.filename)
+  if log_info and log_info.size > 1e9 then
+    local warn_msg = string.format(
+      '%s log is large (%d MB): %s',
+      self.name,
+      log_info.size / (1000 * 1000),
+      self.filename
+    )
+    notify(warn_msg)
+  end
+
+  -- Start message for logging
+  self.logfile:write(
+    string.format('[START][%s] %s logging initiated\n', os.date(log_date_format), self.name)
+  )
+  return true
+end
+
+--- Creates a writer function for a specific log level.
+---
+---@package
+---@param level vim.log.levels
+---@return fun(...:any): boolean? # Returns `false` if the log file could not be opened.
+function Logger:create_writer(level)
+  return function(...)
+    local argc = select('#', ...)
+    if argc == 0 then
+      return true
+    end
+    if not self:open_file() then
+      return false
+    end
+    local message = self.format_func(self.current_level, level, ...)
+    if message then
+      assert(self.logfile)
+      self.logfile:write(message)
+      self.logfile:flush()
+    end
+  end
+end
+
+--- Returns the log filename.
+---
+---@param logger vim.Logger
+---@return string
+function Logger._get_filename(logger)
+  return logger.filename
+end
+
+--- Changes the log file used by the logger.
+---
+---@param logger vim.Logger
+---@param filename string
+function Logger._set_filename(logger, filename)
+  logger.filename = filename
+  logger.openerr = nil
+  if logger.logfile then
+    logger.logfile:close()
+    logger.logfile = nil
+  end
+end
+
+--- Sets the current log level.
+---
+--- Entries below this level are skipped.
+---
+---@param logger vim.Logger
+---@param level vim.log.levels
+function Logger.set_level(logger, level)
+  vim.validate('level', level, { 'string', 'number' })
+  logger.current_level = level
+end
+
+--- Gets the current log level.
+---@param logger vim.Logger
+---@return vim.log.levels
+function Logger.get_level(logger)
+  return logger.current_level
+end
+
+--- Sets the formatter used to produce log entries.
+---
+--- The formatter receives the logger's current level, the message level,
+--- and the values passed to the writer method.
+--- Return a string to write an entry, or `nil` to skip it.
+---
+---@param logger vim.Logger
+---@param func fun(current_level: vim.log.levels, level: vim.log.levels, ...): string?
+function Logger.set_format_func(logger, func)
+  vim.validate('func', func, function(h)
+    return type(h) == 'function' or h == vim.inspect
+  end, false, 'func must be a function')
+
+  logger.format_func = func
+end
+
+--- TODO(ofseed): remove this when `vim.lsp.log.should_log` is removed,
+--- which is the only caller of this function, and it is deprecated.
+---
+---@param logger vim.Logger
+---@param level integer
+---@return boolean
+function Logger._should_log(logger, level)
+  vim.validate('level', level, 'number')
+  return level >= logger.current_level
+end

comment_data.body: Is there a need for such a function? Again, if it's required for (private) lsp functions, I would inline it there until it can be deprecated.

+

+---@nodoc

+  TRACE = 0,
+  DEBUG = 1,
+  INFO = 2,
+  WARN = 3,
+  ERROR = 4,
+  OFF = 5,
+}
+
+local level_names = {
+  [0] = 'TRACE',
+  [1] = 'DEBUG',
+  [2] = 'INFO',
+  [3] = 'WARN',
+  [4] = 'ERROR',

+---

+  if level < current_level then
+    return nil
+  end
+

+  local header = string.format(

+    os.date(log_date_format),
+    info.short_src,
+    info.currentline
+  )
+  local parts = { header }
+  local argc = select('#', ...)
+  for i = 1, argc do

+---

+--- `trace()`, `debug()`, `info()`, `warn()`, and `error()`.
+--- They are correspond to log levels defined by |vim.log.levels|.
+---
+--- Example:
+---
+--- ```lua
+--- local logger = vim.log.new({
+---   name = 'my-plugin',
+---   filename = vim.fs.joinpath(vim.fn.stdpath('log'), 'my-plugin.log'),
+--- })
+---
+--- logger.error('request failed', 'timeout')
+--- -- This will write a line like the following to the log file:
+--- --   [ERROR][2024-01-01 12:00:00] source.lua:123    request failed    timeout
+---
+--- -- Set the log level to `INFO`, otherwise the `info()` call below would be ignored,
+--- -- since the default level is `WARN`.
+--- vim.log.set_level(logger, vim.log.levels.INFO)
+--- logger.info('starting', { buf = vim.api.nvim_get_current_buf() })
+--- -- This will write a line like the following to the log file:
+--- --   [INFO][2024-01-01 12:01:00] source.lua:124    starting    { buf = 1 }
+--- ```
+---
+--- You can also provide a custom formatter function to customize the log output format.
+
+---@class vim.Logger
+---
+--- Display name used in notifications emitted by the logger.
+---@field private name string
+---
+--- Path of the log file.
+---@field private filename string
+---
+--- Minimum level that will be written.
+---@field private current_level integer
+---
+--- Function used to format a log entry.
+---@field private format_func fun(current_level: vim.log.levels, level:vim.log.levels, ...): string?
+---
+--- Internal state for the log file handle. `nil` until the file is opened.
+---@field private logfile file*?
+---
+--- Internal state for the log file open error.
+---@field private openerr string?
+---
+--- Writes a message at `vim.log.levels.TRACE`.
+---@field trace fun(...:any): boolean?
+---
+--- Writes a message at `vim.log.levels.DEBUG`.
+---@field debug fun(...:any): boolean?
+---
+--- Writes a message at `vim.log.levels.INFO`.
+---@field info fun(...:any): boolean?
+---
+--- Writes a message at `vim.log.levels.WARN`.
+---@field warn fun(...:any): boolean?
+---
+--- Writes a message at `vim.log.levels.ERROR`.
+---@field error fun(...:any): boolean?
+local Logger = {}
+
+---@enum vim.log.levels
+---@nodoc
+Logger.levels = {
+  TRACE = 0,
+  DEBUG = 1,
+  INFO = 2,
+  WARN = 3,
+  ERROR = 4,
+  OFF = 5,
+}
+
+local level_names = {
+  [0] = 'TRACE',
+  [1] = 'DEBUG',
+  [2] = 'INFO',
+  [3] = 'WARN',
+  [4] = 'ERROR',
+  [5] = 'OFF',

+--- Default formatter used by |vim.log.new()|.
+---

+---@class vim.log.new.Opts
+---@inlinedoc
+---
+--- Display name used in notifications emitted by the logger.

+  end

+--- Opens the log file on first use.
+---

+---
+---@package
+---@return boolean # `true` if the file is open, `false` on error.
+function Logger:open_file()

+--- Example:

+--- ```lua
+--- local logger = vim.log.new({
+---   name = 'my-plugin',
+---   filename = vim.fs.joinpath(vim.fn.stdpath('log'), 'my-plugin.log'),
+--- })
+---
+--- logger.error('request failed', 'timeout')
+--- -- This will write a line like the following to the log file:
+--- --   [ERROR][2024-01-01 12:00:00] source.lua:123    request failed    timeout
+---
+--- -- Set the log level to `INFO`, otherwise the `info()` call below would be ignored,
+--- -- since the default level is `WARN`.
+--- vim.log.set_level(logger, vim.log.levels.INFO)
+--- logger.info('starting', { buf = vim.api.nvim_get_current_buf() })
+--- -- This will write a line like the following to the log file:
+--- --   [INFO][2024-01-01 12:01:00] source.lua:124    starting    { buf = 1 }
+--- ```
+---
+--- You can also provide a custom formatter function to customize the log output format.
+
+---@class vim.Logger
+---
+--- Display name used in notifications emitted by the logger.
+---@field private name string
+---
+--- Path of the log file.
+---@field private filename string
+---
+--- Minimum level that will be written.
+---@field private current_level integer
+---
+--- Function used to format a log entry.
+---@field private format_func fun(current_level: vim.log.levels, level:vim.log.levels, ...): string?

+---
+--- Internal state for the log file open error.
+---@field private openerr string?
+---
+--- Writes a message at `vim.log.levels.TRACE`.
+---@field trace fun(...:any): boolean?
+---
+--- Writes a message at `vim.log.levels.DEBUG`.
+---@field debug fun(...:any): boolean?
+---
+--- Writes a message at `vim.log.levels.INFO`.
+---@field info fun(...:any): boolean?
+---
+--- Writes a message at `vim.log.levels.WARN`.
+---@field warn fun(...:any): boolean?
+---
+--- Writes a message at `vim.log.levels.ERROR`.
+---@field error fun(...:any): boolean?
+local Logger = {}
+
+---@enum vim.log.levels
+---@nodoc
+Logger.levels = {
+  TRACE = 0,
+  DEBUG = 1,
+  INFO = 2,
+  WARN = 3,
+  ERROR = 4,
+  OFF = 5,
+}
+
+local level_names = {
+  [0] = 'TRACE',
+  [1] = 'DEBUG',
+  [2] = 'INFO',
+  [3] = 'WARN',
+  [4] = 'ERROR',
+  [5] = 'OFF',
+}
+
+local log_date_format = '%F %H:%M:%S'
+
+--- Default formatter used by |vim.log.new()|.
+---
+--- Formats a message as:
+--- `[LEVEL][YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS] source.lua:line<TAB>arg1<TAB>arg2`
+---

+--- Path to the log file.
+---@field filename string

+---
+--- The logger writes formatted messages to a file,
+--- using a per-instance log level and formatting function.
+---

+---@return vim.Logger

+  vim.validate('opts.name', opts.name, 'string')
+  vim.validate('opts.filename', opts.filename, 'string')
+  vim.validate('opts.current_level', opts.current_level, 'number', true)
+  vim.validate('opts.format_func', opts.format_func, 'function', true)
+

+  if log_dir then
+    -- TODO: Ideally the directory should be created in open_file(), right before
+    -- opening the log file, but open() can be called from libuv callbacks,
+    -- where using fn.mkdir() is not allowed.

+  end

+    name = opts.name,
+    filename = opts.filename,
+    current_level = opts.current_level or Logger.levels.WARN,
+    format_func = opts.format_func or default_format_func,
+  }, { __index = Logger })
+  logger.trace = logger:create_writer(Logger.levels.TRACE)
+  logger.debug = logger:create_writer(Logger.levels.DEBUG)
+  logger.info = logger:create_writer(Logger.levels.INFO)
+  logger.warn = logger:create_writer(Logger.levels.WARN)
+  logger.error = logger:create_writer(Logger.levels.ERROR)
+

+end

+---@param msg string
+---@param level? vim.log.levels

+  if vim.in_fast_event() then

+    end)

+    vim.notify(msg, level)
+  end
+end

+---
+--- Writes a `[START]` marker when the file is opened successfully.
+---
+---@package
+---@return boolean # `true` if the file is open, `false` on error.
+function Logger:open_file()

+      self.logfile:write(message)
+      self.logfile:flush()
+    end
+  end
+end
+
+--- Returns the log filename.
+---
+---@param logger vim.Logger
+---@return string
+function Logger._get_filename(logger)


> Is it an idea to bind this to namespaces? So each ns has it's own logger? I can imagine the method-approach then won't be necessary too.

The direct reason I used the current implementation is that our LSP client uses it this way, so I have more confidence in this approach. Personally, I prefer optimizing something that already exists and then making it generic, rather than creating a standard directly from scratch—especially since the issue you mentioned is still under discussion. Looking at the usage of `vim.lsp.log`, it's a perfect example of how to use it; to me, the `vim.log.error(ns, 'error in %s', file)` approach doesn't bring any practical benefit unless we just want to standardize something.

Furthermore, every logger must maintain an open file, so there must be an instance corresponding to it. If we choose to use the `vim.log.error(ns, 'error in %s', file)` approach to make these instances transparent to the outside, it means we would have to maintain instances created by all plugins, which I feel is unnecessary.

> Any reason not to assume (namespace) name = filename? And force `stdpath('log')` as the direction?

This seems to overlap with #38501, and I think it might be better to leave it for that.

> This seems to overlap with https://github.com/neovim/neovim/pull/38501, and I think it might be better to leave it for that.

I don't see these affecting each other at all? At least for the first iteration, I would strongly suggest constraining the log to `stdpath('log')/name.log` (wherever that may be) and leaving configurability for later (based on actual reasonable use requests). I think it's more important to keep all Neovim-related log files together, _especially_ when the base location may change.

The `:log` command, however, may then (better?) be implemented as `vim.log.open('name')`. @superatomic

> every logger must maintain an open file,

Haven't looked at this PR yet, but want to be sure that we keep the door open for abitrary "sinks" in the future. Files are the most obvious "sink", but eventually we will want the ability to define arbitrary function which can route to any sink(s).

And the other very important thing to allow is buffering, i.e. collect many messages before writing them anywhere.

The above might not require any changes to this PR, but let's be sure they will be possible (later) with the interface being introduced here.

> Haven't looked at this PR yet, but want to be sure that we keep the door open for abitrary "sinks" in the future. Files are the most obvious "sink", but eventually we will want the ability to define arbitrary function which can route to any sink(s).
> 
> And the other very important thing to allow is buffering, i.e. collect many messages before writing them anywhere.

To be more precise, what we are maintaining is a `*file` object, which is the return type of Lua's `io.open`. Both `io.stdout` and `io.stderr` are of this type, which should align with the Unix concept of a "file" rather than just a literal file on disk. Buffering is, of course, also supported.

Considering our previous conversation, we should probably change the functionality of passing a `filename` for customization to passing a `*file`, except that this `*file` would default to a file opened based on the `name`, i.e., `stdpath('state') .. '/log/' .. name .. '.log'`.

> approach doesn't bring any practical benefit unless we just want to standardize something.

I proposed it because of the first point of [:h dev-lua](https://neovim.io/doc/user/dev/#dev-lua):

> Keep the core Lua modules lua-stdlib simple. Avoid elaborate OOP or pseudo-OOP designs. Plugin authors just want functions to call, not a big, fancy inheritance hierarchy.

> The `:log` command, however, may then (better?) be implemented as `vim.log.open('name')`.

Makes sense. Once this PR and my `:log` PR are merged, I can open another one that moves most of the command logic to a new `vim.log.open()`. It will lack some features compared to `:log` though, unless `mods` are passed in.

Edit: I've done some more thinking about it, and I'm not actually sure if we need this. Users can just call `vim.cmd.log(name)`.

> based on the `name`, i.e., `stdpath('state') .. '/log/' .. name .. '.log'`.

Should be `stdpath('log') .. '/' .. name .. '.log'`.

> the `vim.log.error(ns, 'error in %s', file)` approach doesn't bring any practical benefit unless we just want to standardize something.

getting a standard interface is the first step. also,

- at least one practical benefit is performance: the printf-style formatting avoids the formatting cost if a log-level isn't enabled.
- the door is open to more elaborate ideas later, which may involve a long-lived `logger` object

> Edit: I've done some more thinking about it, and I'm not actually sure if we need this. Users can just call vim.cmd.log(name).

I meant as an alternative to the Ex command, for two reasons:
1. much(!) easier to implement and iterate (pure Lua instead of C)
2. keeping related API together (both interface and implementation).

(This does not preclude adding a `:Log` wrapper like we do for the various `:Inspect*` functions, which I see as being on the same level.)

> And the other very important thing to allow is buffering, i.e. collect many messages before writing them anywhere.

Gitsigns collects a lot of log messages but keeps them in memory and are not persistent to each nvim session, which keeps it fast and prevents it from overfilling a persistent log file.

Is something like this in scope. Writing all logs to file just isn't sensible for gitsigns. Nor do I think it is for LSP tbh.

>  Nor do I think it is for LSP tbh.

This is getting a bit off-topic, but very much seconded. Not a few servers just write stream-of-conciousness to `stderr`, which often leaves me with multi-megabyte logfiles after a few days (and is apparently allowed by the spec...)

> I proposed it because of the first point of [:h dev-lua](https://neovim.io/doc/user/dev/#dev-lua):

This API is also not intended to be called in an OOP style, even though the documentation might make it appear so.

Assuming a user has a `logger`, the way to call various writers would be `logger.error()`; for `get_level`, my ideal calling convention should be `vim.log.get_level(logger)`, rather than just `log:get_level()`. This is similar to Lua's built-in types, such as `string`, which can be accessed via both `string.match(s, pattern)` and `s:match(pattern)`. I have explained this type of API design in [other PR](https://github.com/neovim/neovim/pull/38869#issuecomment-4212572718); we simply need to modify `gen_vimdoc.lua`.

> getting a standard interface is the first step

Considering that the current API does not conflict with this approach, I think we can proceed with this PR for now.

> Writing all logs to file just isn't sensible for gitsigns. Nor do I think it is for LSP tbh.

Regarding the log output location, combined with the discussion above, I think the best course of action for now is to avoid exposing `filename` and only provide files located at `stdpath('log') .. 'name' .. '.log'`. This avoids committing to "outputting logs to a specific file" as an API guarantee at this stage, and we can show the logs by the proposed `:log` command.

> much(!) easier to implement and iterate (pure Lua instead of C)

`:log` is written in Lua.

> an alternative to the Ex command

It's main purpose is to be interactive. I don't really think we need a Lua API for this, especially when it would lose features compared to the Ex command.

> Any reason not to assume (namespace) name = filename? And force `stdpath('log')` as the direction?

I avoided exposing `filename` because it is an internal implementation detail, so this is currently how it works.

Regarding the discussion above, I have summarized two (potential) points for improvement.

1. Controlling the log output location, or implementing buffering
The current writer is based on Lua's `io` module, which allows for buffering; since we avoid exposing the output location externally, the internal implementation can still be changed in the future.
2. Standardize plugin namespaces, using `vim.log.error({namespace}, what)` instead of `logger.error(what)`
We currently expose very few APIs. For functions like `logger.error`, logging would be more convenient; for functions like `vim.log.get_level({logger})`, we could support calls like `vim.log.get_level({name})` through overloading, so I think this can also be improved later. However, since every logger corresponds to a file or buffer, we need to prioritize resolving the first point regarding its implementation; furthermore, managing the state of all plugins is quite cumbersome.

In summary, I believe we can still move forward with this PR.

Something to look into in the future: viewing a log with `:log name` should flush the write buffer, so the logs are fully up to date. This is only needed if it's possible for the logs to be delayed by a noticible amount (I haven't looked at the PR in detail so I don't know if this will be an issue).
I have created a PR regarding my previous suggestion to have the documentation emphasize calling it in the form of `vim.log.set_level(logger, level)` rather than `logger:set_level` #39078.
Since #39078 was merged, I believe the API doc here looks much better. I've added tests for this new feature (AI-assisted). This PR should be ready to merge.

I don't necessarily think this should block this PR but I have some concerns with this implementation. I'm very opinionated with what logging frameworks I pick for projects. Most of my concerns are regarding how coupled this implementation is to the file sink and that it may be difficult to do incremental improvements to what I personally look for in a logging framework.

1. I want full control over what gets logged and where it goes. I don't want plugins creating a bunch of log files that I can't override.
2. I want to be able to swap out and choose different sinks depending on the log source.
3. I should be able to create my own sinks

For example I would like to be able to do in my own dotfiles is set up a date rolling file sink that creates a new file each day up to a maximum of thirty days and after any older files get deleted and I want limits on file size. Logs from all sources except lsp should go to this sink. I then would like a seperate sink dedicated to only lsp since it is the noisiest. I would also like to have a sink that outputs to vim.notify that I can enable when debugging my config.

I believe that this PRs implementation will result in plugin authors creating tons of log files dedicated to each plugin without end user customization which is basically the current plugin landscape. This PR has the benefit over current landscape in that it at least standardizes the implementation.

In my opinion the logger new function should be replaced with a vim.log.enable function with all the sinks, filters, etc configured in the options.

(I reviewed this on my phone so if I said something incorrect then thats probably why)

> I want full control over what gets logged and where it goes. I don't want plugins creating a bunch of log files that I can't override.

The log location follows nvim standards (`:h standard-path`), so customizing the log path will always be limited. However, the benefit is that you can indeed control the location of all logs via `$NVIM_LOG_FILE`. One benefit of this is that the `:log` command was previously added to open logs, thereby unifying log management. 

> I want to be able to swap out and choose different sinks depending on the log source.

Regarding sinks, there has been much discussion above. The earliest version of this PR allowed for fully custom output path via a `filename`. In addition to removing it for the reasons mentioned above, we are also considering support not only for file-based logging. Furthermore, we may later change the log output to be stored in memory and only written to a file when necessary for performance reasons. This part is explicitly TBD.

> In my opinion the logger new function should be replaced with a vim.log.enable function with all the sinks, filters, etc configured in the options.

The current implementation already allows for filter configuration. For example, you can filter log levels via `set_level`, and full customization is possible through `format_func`.

The current implementation of this PR is refactored from the original LSP log rather than writing a new one from scratch, so more features are left for follow-ups.

Looking at this some more I think I can see how this could incrementally move towards configuring sinks. Rereading the comments I see why you went with vim.log.new as it allows plugin authors to do an easy replacement.  `local log = vim.log.new and vim.log.new({...}) or require('myplugin.log')`

This makes sense and I think y'all are correct that that is the best approach. I think that the format_func and current_level as options are also good since plugin authors can set defaults. I also like that you can override those defaults with set_format_func and set_level although I personally would prefer this to be configured with a vim.log.enable function instead.  Although maybe you do want to be able set those on the fly without changing anything else with `:lua vim.log.set_level('lsp', vim.log.levels.ERROR`. I need to think that part through some more. I think a `vim.log.enable(enable, opts)` would be the best incremetal improvement to this PR. Where it uses the default sink when no options are supplied. The default sink would be how you currently have the logging configured except it would need to be refactored out as it is currently tightly coupled to the logger. The options would then let you add different sinks and configure it so that for example neotest logs goes to default file sink while vim.lsp logs goes to my custom rolling log sink. Also format_func really should be a sink configuration rather than a logger configuration since not all sinks will want the same format for example you may have json file sink. 

Some responses:

> The log location follows nvim standards (:h standard-path), so customizing the log path will always be limited. However, the benefit is that you can indeed control the location of all logs via $NVIM_LOG_FILE. One benefit of this is that the :log command was previously added to open logs, thereby unifying log management.

True and I don't think my suggestion goes against this as the default sink (and probably all custom file sinks) should respect $NVIM_LOG_FILE

> The current implementation already allows for filter configuration. For example, you can filter log levels via set_level, and full customization is possible through format_func.

Yeah I like this but I do feel like at least format_func should probably be per sink configuration rather than logger source configurations (maybe this could be handled with an opts parameter). I think that format_func is the most likely function to experience churn when sinks are added, and the fact that it does both filtering and formatting also makes the likelyhood of churn higher.

> However, the benefit is that you can indeed control the location of all logs via `$NVIM_LOG_FILE`.

This only works for Nvim's own log file, and not any other third-party log files (or the first-party `lsp.log` and `nvim-pack.log`). I believe @justinmk also said that `$NVIM_LOG_FILE` is not a pattern we want to replicate.
